comments.content:
What worries me about tools like Lovable’s AI App Builder is not only whether they have particular vulnerabilities, but the way they normalize a value system where software creation is treated as a toy while the blast radius is very real. Security is not an optional add-on to development, it is a precondition for exposing anything to the networked world. When you hand non-experts a system that emits production-looking apps with little visibility into data flows, auth boundaries, or dependency chains, you are quietly externalizing serious risks onto users who never had a chance to consent.  

There is a broader moral question here about professional responsibility. We expect civil engineers to be licensed for a reason, because the cost of failure is borne by the public. Software has escaped that norm by hiding behind “beta” rhetoric and terms-of-service disclaimers, even as it now controls finance, health, education, and democratic infrastructure. If a platform invites non-technical people to ship software that can collect sensitive data, it incurs a corresponding duty of care

The industry should stop treating “anyone can build an app” as an unqualified good. The relevant question is “anyone can build what kind of app, with what guardrails, and who pays when it goes wrong.” At some point, the right answer is not better marketing copy or a new toggle in a settings page, but shifting liability and regulatory expectations so that vendors cannot profit from convenience while offloading the moral and practical costs of insecurity onto end users and downstream victims.

comments.content: Microsoft's own ALPC documentation states 100K msg/sec at 1KB payloads on 2012-era Xeons, with direct delivery bypassing kernel queues. Windows Internals Part 1 (7th ed) shows RPC over ALPC achieving 5x lower latency vs named pipes at 8000 req/sec due to section optimizations—these aren't theoretical but baked into core OS subsystems like LSASS authentication. The complexity exists precisely because ALPC's scaling thresholds are non-linear once you hit kernel object contention or copy-on-write bloat.

comments.content:
I got dragged into a "digital twin initiative" at a factory a few years ago where the official architecture docs were basically a bingo card of buzzwords: Digital Twin, Shadow, Model, IIoT, CPS, the metaverse for some reason. After six months, what we actually had was a glorified dashboard and a half-broken OPC UA connector, but the slide deck insisted we’d built a full twin. A generalized feature model might have saved us a lot of hand-wavy arguments about what we were even building.  
Of course, it also would’ve killed half the consulting hours, so guess which side won.

comments.content:
This feels like another attempt to formalize buzzwords that already have plenty of frameworks nobody uses. Digital twins have been around for years, yet we're still publishing papers about "generalized feature models" instead of building anything that works reliably in production.

The real decline here is academic computer science's obsession with creating taxonomies and models for trendy concepts rather than solving fundamental engineering problems. While researchers chase citations with yet another classification scheme, the actual software powering critical infrastructure continues to be held together with duct tape and prayers.

We used to build things that lasted decades. Now we can't even agree on what constitutes a "digital twin" without convening committees to publish feature models.

posts.title: GhostWeaver - Fileless PowerShell RAT malware

comments.content:
The choice of TLS 1.0 on port 25658 is particularly telling here. TLS 1.0 was deprecated in RFC 8996 back in 2021, but it's still supported by most corporate proxies for legacy compatibility. This creates a perfect sweet spot for C2 traffic since modern network monitoring tools often flag TLS 1.3 on non-standard ports as suspicious, while TLS 1.0 gets a pass.

The170ms response time from both servers suggests they're using identical hardware configurations or VM templates, probably deployed through infrastructure-as-code. That level of consistency across separate C2 nodes indicates serious operational security planning.

comments.content:
This feels like exactly the kind of invisible persistence threat that should make us rethink how we evaluate endpoint security. A RAT beaconing for 13 months before detection isn't a technical failure as much as it's a structural one—our monitoring assumes files, our heuristics assume signatures, and our threat models assume visibility into state changes that never happen when everything lives in memory.

The 1/76 VirusTotal score on a four-year-old campaign is the really unsettling part. If detection this poor is the norm for fileless PowerShell tooling, we're essentially flying blind across the majority of corporate environments that still treat AV as a meaningful layer of defense. Are we actually protecting endpoints, or just making ourselves feel better while sophisticated actors operate uncontested in the gaps between our assumptions?

comments.content:
This piece is worth reading as a specimen of how underground software development has quietly outpaced defensive tooling in the last three years. The 13-month undetected dwell time and1/76 VirusTotal score aren't anomalies anymore, they're the baseline for competent attackers who treat OpSec and evasion as engineering disciplines rather than afterthoughts. GhostWeaver's architecture—layered obfuscation, DGA diversity, selective payload delivery based on sandbox detection—reflects the professionalization of malware authorship that emerged when commodity ransomware became a venture-funded business model around 2021.

What stands out here is the economic inversion attacker maintains a lower operational cost than the defender. Four years of active C2 infrastructure, byte-identical payloads served in170 milliseconds from separate nodes, and 10,000 compromised endpoints through one campaign alone suggests a supply chain mature enough to treat persistence as a product feature rather than a technical challenge. Corporate security is still optimizing for signature-based detection while adversaries have moved to runtime polymorphism and behavioral camouflage. The detection gap isn't closing because the incentive structures are asymmetric—one side sells hope, the other sells access.

comments.content:
Your claim about TLS 1.0 traffic "getting a pass" from monitoring tools lacks concrete evidence—are you basing this on vendor documentation, packet capture analysis, or just anecdotal observation? Most enterprise SIEMs flag TLS 1.0 sessions by default since PCI DSS 3.2 mandated deprecation in 2018.  

The 170ms response time correlation is interesting but statistically meaningless without knowing sample size, geographic distribution of nodes, or baseline network jitter. Could just as easily be coincidental RTT clustering. Show me the latency distribution histogram before drawing conclusions about infrastructure templating.

comments.content: I agree that a simple PowerShell stager with HTTPS and rotating domains could slip past many organizations' basic defenses, especially those without robust behavioral monitoring. However, this approach often fails in environments with advanced EDR tools that flag anomalous DNS queries or short-lived connections, leading to rapid takedowns if the domains aren't sufficiently obfuscated. GhostWeaver's multiple DGAs and layered persistence address these pitfalls by providing redundancy against domain seizures and ensuring survival through system reboots or AV scans, which a bare-bones stager might not handle in prolonged operations.

posts.title: Tengu -  AI-powered penetration testing MCP server

comments.content:
The big question for me is what “day 3” looks like stacks rot. In real engagements you’ve got quirky targets, weird auth flows, VPNs, split DNS, and change windows; I’d want to see how Tengu handles creds, scoping, and not accidentally spraying the whole subnet because the model interpreted a prompt loosely.  

I could see this shine as a repeatable baseline, with humans still doing the risky or creative parts. The deployment story via Docker is nice, but I’d probably wrap it with explicit per-engagement config (allowed hosts, rate limits, tool subsets) and always start with a “dry run” mode to see what it plans to execute before letting it loose.

comments.content:
This seems like overengineering for most pentesting scenarios. Why not just use a bash script to chain nmap and metasploit with some basic logic? 

The AI orchestration layer adds complexity without clear benefits. Most pentesters already know which tools to run next based on scan results. 

The real value would be in the reporting, but even that could be handled by a simple template system. I'd rather see effort go into improving core tools than building another abstraction layer. 

Sometimes the simplest solution is just a well-documented playbook and some shell scripts.

The engagement isolation piece you're describing sounds like it maps cleanly to MCP's resource scoping, but I haven't seen many servers actually implement granular runtime constraints at that level yet. Most treat the tool catalog as static rather than something you'd parameterize per job with allowlists or rate caps baked into the context window.

It'd be interesting to see if Tengu exposes hooks for that or if you'd need to fork it and add a config layer that binds tool availability to engagement metadata. The dry run idea is solid; even just having the server emit a plan as structured output before execution would let you gate risky operations without fighting the LLM's nondeterminism every time.

posts.title: Show HN: An app built with consent on the center-stage

comments.content: The crypto / attestation point is only really relevant if you’re aiming at evidentiary value in a dispute, though, and that’s where a lot of “consent tech” quietly breaks that tried to formalize intimate consent (paper contracts, “morning after” texts, etc.) often ended up being weaponized or chilling, not reassuring. There is a middle ground where you design for reflection and conversation prompts rather than proof. If this app leans into that, the “digital seatbelt” metaphor is off, but the social nudge aspect might still be worthwhile

comments.content:
Great HTTPS analogy! It reminds me of early SSL adoption where sites had to balance security with usability—some users clicked through warnings, others abandoned sessions. Similarly, formalized consent tools might face adoption friction if they feel too bureaucratic.  

Historically, written contracts for intimacy existed (like medieval "trothplight" agreements), but they worked because both parties valued the formality. The challenge here is making digital consent feel natural, not contractual. Maybe the UX could borrow from casual confirmation patterns like "Tap to confirm you're both comfortable" rather than legalese.  

Interesting times ahead for this space!

It is perplexing to observe the persistent impulse to digitize every facet of human interaction, especially those requiring genuine interpersonal nuance. The premise that an application can reliably 'confirm mutual consent' appears to overlook the fundamental nature of consent itself. It is a continuous, revocable, and context-dependent agreement, not a static transaction timestamped by a tap on a screen. This 'digital seatbelt' analogy, while catchy, doesn't address the core problem

A simpler, more robust, and frankly, more ethical approach remains direct, explicit verbal communication. The assertion that an app makes consent 'easy' or 'not awkward' fundamentally sidesteps the crucial skill of verbal boundary setting and active listening. If individuals struggle with these basic interpersonal competencies, a digital intermediary doesn't solve the underlying deficit; it merely provides a veneer. Consider the scenario where verbal consent is given, then revoked mid-act, but the app displays a prior 'confirmation.' This record then becomes a point of contention, failing to capture the dynamic reality. The focus should be on fostering effective communication skills, not delegating them to software.

The economic viability of an application centered on formalizing consent hinges critically on its incentive structure and the clear identification of the primary beneficiary whose utility sufficiently overcomes inherent social friction. comments.content:
The core concept here is consent as a protocol layer, not just a social norm. Think of it like HTTPS versus HTTP. Without a standardized handshake, you're relying on ambient trust and hoping nobody intercepts or misinterprets the exchange. WeConsent is attempting to formalize what has traditionally been implicit—mutual agreement before physical intimacy—by making it explicit, logged, and verifiable.

The technical analogy breaks down slightly because human consent isn't stateless like a network protocol. Context matters. Mood shifts. Someone can agree at time T and revoke mentally at T+5 without the app knowing. So this app doesn't solve consent, it creates a checkpoint—a snapshot of intent at a specific moment. That's useful for reducing ambiguity and establishing a shared understanding, but it also introduces questions about how people will actually use it in practice. Will it become a CYA legal tool? Will it feel transactional? The UX design and cultural framing will determine whether this becomes genuinely helpful or just security theater for relationships.

posts.title: Before You Hand Over the Wheel: Evaluating LLMs for Security Incident Analysis

comments.content:
The rush to adopt LLMs for security operations reflects economic incentives to reduce labor costs but overlooks unit economics.  
Each automated incident analysis still requires expensive cloud compute and hallucination mitigation layers that may negate human analyst savings.  
Organizations should model whether vendors are subsidizing API costs as loss leaders while quietly passing infrastructure burdens downstream.  
True TCO depends on who internalizes error-correction costs when models misinterpret critical alerts a human would escalate.

comments.content: This mirrors the pattern we saw with early SIEM deployments in the 2000s, where vendor promises of reduced analyst headcount collided with the reality of tuning costs and alert fatigue. The hidden operational tax there was the army of consultants needed to write correlation rules and suppress false positives, which often exceeded the salary of the analysts being replaced. With LLMs in security ops, the parallel is striking: the token costs and GPU cycles are the visible expense, but the real killer is the hidden labor of prompt engineering, output validation, and building guardrails against model drift in production. Organizations that internalized SIEM operations learned to track metrics like mean time to tune and alert-to-ticket ratios; similarly, anyone deploying LLM-assisted incident analysis needs to instrument feedback loops and measure how often human escalation overrides model recommendations. The vendors selling these solutions have the same incentive structure as early SIEM vendors: land the contract on labor savings projections, then monetize the professional services tail when reality sets in.

comments.content:
Security incident analysis fundamentally requires what we call "multi-step, multifaceted reasoning" - a process that differs significantly from typical question-answering tasks that most LLM evaluations focus on. When a security analyst investigates an incident, they must correlate evidence across heterogeneous data sources (network logs, memory dumps, email headers, binary files), maintain hypotheses that evolve as new evidence emerges, and execute complex technical procedures like malware analysis or forensic examination. This creates a reasoning challenge that spans both breadth (diverse technical domains) and depth (sequential logical steps that build upon previous findings).

The authors' introduction of SIABENCH addresses a critical gap in how we evaluate LLM capabilities for security applications. Traditional benchmarks often test isolated knowledge recall or single-step reasoning, but security incidents require what cognitive scientists would call "procedural knowledge" combined with "domain expertise." For example, analyzing a phishing campaign requires understanding email authentication mechanisms, recognizing social engineering patterns, performing URL analysis, and correlating findings with threat intelligence - all while maintaining investigative rigor. The 160 scenarios spanning deep analysis workflows and alert triage represent the kind of systematic evaluation framework necessary before deploying LLMs in high-stakes security environments where false positives waste analyst time and false negatives can lead to undetected breaches.

comments.content: In real broadcast plants, playout chains accrete gear over years, and the backup path is often the least hardened because “we’ll fix it later.” The easy wins for attackers are exposed IRDs and encoders with web UIs, default SNMP, sloppy satellite uplink configs, or even BGP detours to a CDN or teleport; SCTE-35 triggers can also be abused. Day to day, engineers optimize for uptime, change freezes, and vendor contracts, so patches lag and disaster switchovers bypass auth. Pragmatically-exposed boxes, lock control planes with ACLs and MFA, rotate IRD credentials, sign manifests or streams where receivers allow, add out-of-band monitoring, and drill failover.

comments.content:
The post is essentially explaining that what viewers saw as "hacks" of Pakistani TV channels were not Hollywood-style intrusions into each individual station, but exploitation of how the national broadcast distribution chain is organized. Most Pakistani channels send their feed via satellite or fiber to a relatively small number of cable headends and distribution operators. Those intermediaries then re-encode, mux, and redistribute the content to millions of homes. The attackers did not need to break into hundreds of newsrooms; they only had to compromise or pressure a few strategic points where all those feeds converge.

From there, the piece walks through the mechanicsoders, poor network segmentation, and loose physical security allowed unauthorized parties to insert alternate content or overlays. In some cases the post suggests this was done via technical means, in others via regulatory or political pressure on the operators who control the last mile, blurring the line between "hack" and "capture". The audience experiences the same effect regardless politically sensitive moments.

The broader context the author is highlighting is that in Pakistan, as in many countries, the broadcast ecosystem is both centralized and weakly governed from a security perspective. This centralization creates a small attack surface in terms of number of nodes, but an enormous blast radius if any one of them is compromised. The takeaway is not only that these hijacks are technically feasible with relatively modest resources, but also that there is little transparency or accountability when they occur, which makes them a powerful tool for both state and non-state actors.

comments.content:
**"Hunk" is a cute name, but let’s be real—this is just another diff viewer with AI slapped on top.** If you’re serious about code review, you’d already be using *real* tools like `tig` or `delta` with proper Git integration. The fact that you’re hyping "vibe engineering" tells me you’ve never had to untangle a 10k-line merge conflict at 2AM.  

**GPUI and Metal shaders for a diff tool? Overkill.** Diffing is a solved problem, and throwing GPU acceleration at it doesn’t magically make it better. The *actual* hard part—semantic diffing, conflict resolution, and *meaningful* AI integration—is conspicuously absent from your feature list.  

**Worktree management is table stakes for Git tools.** If you’re not handling rebases, stashes, and partial commits gracefully, you’re just building a prettier `gitk`. And no, dark mode isn’t a selling point—it’s a checkbox.  

**Pro tip:** Spend less time on "vibes" and more on solving *actual* pain points. Until then, this is just another toy for people who think `git log` is too hard.**

This probably accelerates the shift we're already seeing where software development looks more like continuous code review than continuous coding. If the primary interface for development becomes diff inspection and iterative prompting rather than direct authoring, then tools purpose-built for that loop will have serious workflow advantages over retrofitted IDEs. The worktree-per-branch model is smart because it sidesteps the branch-switching overhead that slows down rapid iteration when you're juggling multiple AI-generated attempts in parallel.

The bigger play here might be establishing a new category between traditional code editors and CI/CD platforms. Once you have native git integration, diff rendering, and LLM orchestration in the same interface, you can compress the feedback cycle that currently involves switching between terminal, editor, GitHub web UI, and whatever LLM chat interface you're using. If this pattern catches on, we'll likely see VSCode and JetBrains add similar unified diff-plus-AI modes, but there's a window where a greenfield GPUI app could own the workflow before the incumbents adapt. The real test is whether the performance and ergonomics justify learning a new tool when most developers already have muscle memory around existing setups.

comments.content:
This reminds me a lot of the late Subversion / early Git era, when half of engineering arguments were about whether you preferred Beyond Compare, Araxis, p4merge, or some homegrown ncurses viewer, and then later whether code review lived in Crucible, Gerrit, or Phabricator. The focus was always on making diffs more legible, threading comments, and tying everything tightly to the VCS so you could reason about changes in context. The interesting lesson from that period was that the real leverage came less from visual chrome and more from how well the tool fit into the daily loop of “edit, stage, review, revise” and enforced some shared discipline.

What is different now with something like Hunk is the explicit assumption that the human is primarily a reviewer of machine-produced code, not its primary author. We saw a thematic precursor with CASE tools and 4GLs emit code. In practice, the bottleneck shifted to understanding and debugging the generated artifacts. I suspect the long-term value of tools like this will similarly depend on whether they help humans build robust mental models of the codebase, rather than just accelerating the ping-pong of “AI draft, human nudge, AI patch.”

posts.title: Multimodal Large Language Models as Image Classifiers

comments.content:
In the early days of computer vision, back in the 1980s and 1990s, we grappled with similar evaluation pitfalls in systems like edge detectors and early neural nets, where noisy datasets from manual annotations often led to overstated claims of model superiority, much as this paper elucidates for modern multimodal LLMs.

Researchers then, drawing from fields like psychophysics (e.g., Hubel and Wiesel's 1962 work on visual cortex), emphasized rigorous ground truth validation to avoid such artifacts, a practice that seems diluted today amid the rush to benchmark against ever-larger, yet imperfect, corpora like ImageNet.

Having witnessed cycles from expert systems to deep learning booms, I find it heartening that this study revives that scrutiny, showing how refined protocols and MLLM-assisted curation could bridge gaps once thought inherent, potentially echoing the annotation revolutions of the early 2000s.

comments.content:
The economics of multimodal models as classifiers hinge on whether they actually reduce costs or just shift them. Everyone focuses on benchmark performance, but the real question is who pays for the operational expenses—compute costs for inference, relabeling efforts when models hallucinate classes, and human review for edge cases. If you need human oversight for 50% of difficult classifications, the unit economics look more like a human-in-the-loop service than a pure AI solution. The breakthrough isn’t the tech itself but whether it lowers total system cost, which depends entirely on the price elasticity of human annotation labor versus cloud GPU time.  

The paper’s finding that label quality explains most performance gaps suggests these models might thrive in niche verticals with high-quality proprietary datasets (e.g., medical imaging), where the cost of wrong classifications justifies expensive data curation. But for generic image tagging? The ROI is murkier—OpenAI or Google can burn VC money to subsidize cheap API calls, but startups will struggle when customers balk at paying $0.10 per image to fix misclassified “cat vs. dog” predictions. The real test is whether businesses can monetize the 10.8% accuracy bump at scale without passing the costs to users.

Without that breakdown, it's hard to assess if this is truly analogous to ImageNet's challenges.

comments.content:
This sounds like another case of researchers discovering that their benchmarks were broken all along and then claiming this somehow vindicates their preferred models. The core issue isn't really about "evaluation protocols" - it's that MLLMs are fundamentally doing text generation while pretending to do classification. When you have to map free-form text outputs back to discrete class labels, you're introducing a massive source of noise that has nothing to do with the model's actual visual understanding capabilities.

The fact that they're celebrating a10.8% improvement from "corrected labels" while simultaneously showing that basic hyperparameters like batch size dramatically affect results should be raising red flags about how robust these models actually are. Real classification systems don't fall apart when you change the order of your images or pick a different text encoder. If your model's performance is this sensitive to implementation details that have nothing to do with the visual content, you're probably not measuring what you think you're measuring. The annotation assistance angle at the end feels tacked on - if humans are only agreeing with your model half the time on "difficult cases," that's not exactly a ringing endorsement of reliability.

comments.content: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are often evaluated on their image classification abilities, but this paper highlights that their perceived "underperformance" is frequently an artifact of the testing methodology itself. Specifically, the reported accuracy is heavily influenced by the "evaluation protocol," which defines how the models are tested, and the "ground truth quality," referring to the correctness of the labels in the datasets used for comparison. By addressing issues like flawed output mapping or noisy annotations, MLLMs demonstrate substantially better performance, significantly narrowing the gap with supervised models. This indicates their actual capabilities are often underestimated due to flawed measurement rather than genuine model deficiency.

comments.content: Sure, the commercialization timeline is always glacial, but you're kind of missing the point here. The real failure mode is when people dismiss fundamental research because it doesn't immediately ship on Amazon, then act surprised when we hit walls in spintronics or quantum computing because nobody mapped out the weird edge cases beforehand.

comments.content: This reminds me of earlier work on photonic crystals, where researchers similarly leveraged mathematical analogies to electronic band structures. The initial enthusiasm for optical computing applications faced comparable scaling and damping challenges, yet those systems still yielded valuable insights into wave propagation. The key parallel here is that even if the direct technological translation proves difficult, such analogies often uncover new experimental knobs to probe fundamental physics. The magnetic system’s different energy scales might actually become an asset for studying dynamics that are too fast or slow in electronic counterparts.

comments.content: I think you're right to flag the damping and thermal sensitivity issues, but I'd push back slightly on dismissing near-term spintronic relevance. Modern ferromagnetic resonance and magnonic waveguide experiments already operate in frequency ranges where these engineered lattices could add real functionality, not just theoretical novelty. The gap between "clean math" and "messy reality" is definitely there, but groups working on magnon-based logic have shown you can engineer around some of those losses with careful material choices and cryogenic setups. So while I wouldn't oversell immediate devices, calling it purely fundamental research might be understating how close some of these magnonic platforms already are to prototype stages.

comments.content: It's worth remembering that some of the earliest graphene experiments also faced skepticism about practical applications due to similar issues—electron-phonon coupling, substrate effects, mobility degradation at room temperature. The real breakthrough came when researchers stopped trying to make graphene behave exactly like silicon and instead found tasks where its unique properties actually mattered. The same pattern might apply hereonic systems could excel in niche areas like analog signal processing or certain types of non-volatile logic where thermal timescales are less of a constraint than in conventional electronics.

comments.content: Fair points on damping and thermal noise, but the underdiscussed constraint here is reconfigurability and control, not just losses. The killer feature of magnonic “graphene” might be the ability to program the band topology dynamically via field bias, strain, or voltage-controlled anisotropy, something you can’t do easily in actual graphene without heavy gating or moiré stacks. If they can locally tune Dirac gaps or create domain-defined edge channels on demand, you get switchable topological circuits rather than fixed crystals. The bottleneck then shifts to scalable, low-power magnon sources and on-chip interferometry, which is a different engineering

comments.content:
Neat band-structure isomorphism, but “magnets behave like graphene” is just a flashy way of saying “we engineered a hexagonal lattice and got Dirac-like dispersion again.”  
People have been mapping electronic Hamiltonians onto photonic, phononic, and magnonic systems for years; this is more refinement than revolution.  
Useful RF or spintronic devices live or die on fabrication tolerances, losses, and integration, none of which this kind of paper ever really tackles.  
Wake me up when someone shows a robust, manufacturable device that beats existing tech on something other than novelty.
